description: A 'kisholu' is an explicit set of technical requirements for an artifact, process, or service. It can say what a bridge must carry or how a component must fit. 'kirema' is one criterion, 'norae' a method, and 'kirothe' the act of defining; a specification gathers those requirements. Whether they are current and legitimate remains open.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'. Raise the tongue blade behind the alveolar ridge for stressed unvoiced 'sh', pass air along the groove, and round into true-mid back 'o'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let air pass along its sides, and finish with high rounded back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: Crisp 'ki' opens into the wider hush and round vowel of stressed 'sho', then continues through liquid 'lu'. A precise point broadens into a set and leaves room for more than one requirement.
usage_notes: The noun is countable. Exact values, units, identifiers, and versions remain in the source record, along with any quoted requirement. Ordinary 'heno' can say that a system satisfies a specification. Certification and safety require their own grounds.

pillars.solarpunk-values: An open specification can make shared infrastructure easier to inspect and repair. Its authors and consequences remain open to challenge.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A requirement can be quoted without turning its technical wording into unquestionable command.

semantic_domains.cognition: a reference against which properties and performance may be assessed
semantic_domains.communication: a shareable statement of requirements that may still be disputed or revised
semantic_domains.creation: an explicit set of requirements for design, work, or technical provision
